tasks.description: Close the edge-orientation ambiguity class (FGAP-113): make edge orientation explicit metadata on every orientation-bearing relation_type and consume it symmetrically so a wrong-orientation guess is never silent on either side. WRITE — a backwards orientation-bearing edge is REJECTED at append (never auto-oriented; the reverted 2026-06-06 always-swap inverted canonically-authored edges, a08e586). READ — a walk/query issued against the wrong end of an orientation-bearing relation ERRORS (or normalizes with an explicit signal) rather than returning silent-empty. Covers ordering relations (task_depends_on_task and its same-kind siblings), the membership relations (reconciling the contains/includes vs positioned_in container-orientation split), and the unconstrained wildcard item_derived_from_item; reconciles relation_type names/metadata with the deriver contract; migrates any inverted edges. FGAP-007 (task_depends_on_task) is the write-ordering instance; the task_positioned_in_phase silent-empty is the read instance.
acceptance_criteria: Every orientation-bearing relation_type carries explicit endpoint-role/orientation metadata (extending the existing source_kinds/target_kinds) naming which stored endpoint holds which semantic role: the ordering relations (task_depends_on_task, story_depends_on_story, feature_depends_on_item, feature_gated_by_item, story_gated_by_item, requirement_depends_on_requirement, milestone_precedes_milestone, decision_supersedes_decision, research_supersedes_research), the membership relations (feature_contains_story, story_includes_item, task_positioned_in_phase, phase_positioned_in_milestone), and the wildcard item_derived_from_item — each either carries the metadata or is demonstrably exempt because it has no consumed orientation semantics; none is left with a name-vs-deriver or name-vs-container inversion.

WRITE enforcement: append-relation/append-relations REJECT a backwards orientation-bearing edge at write time with a message naming the expected orientation — a direction-correct edge is accepted, a backwards one is rejected, never silently stored and never auto-oriented. The rejection fires only for relation_types carrying the orientation metadata; relations without it are unaffected (no new false-positive rejections). Provable per relation_type: a name-faithful-but-backwards append is rejected; the canonical-direction append succeeds.

WRITE enforcement covers the wildcard: item_derived_from_item (source/target [*], no kind pin) is oriented by its declared role metadata, not by the absent kind check — a backwards item_derived_from_item append is rejected.

READ signaling: a walk-ancestors/walk-descendants (and any walk porcelain) issued against the WRONG end of an orientation-bearing relation ERRORS or returns an explicit wrong-orientation signal — not a bare empty array indistinguishable from a genuine absence. Provable: querying task_positioned_in_phase from the member end vs the container end yields distinguishable results (one the membership, the other an explicit wrong-direction signal), never two silently-different empties.

Membership orientation is reconciled to internal consistency: the container-orientation split (contains/includes store container=parent while positioned_in stores container=child) is resolved or the metadata explicitly encodes each so no learned heuristic is silently wrong for a sibling; documented on the relation_type surface.

Every existing edge in relations.json remains valid after the fix and any migration: none orphaned, dropped, or left inverted; any edge whose stored direction is backwards under the corrected contract is migrated to correct orientation; already-correct edges are unchanged.
The deriver's blocked/ready and lineage output is unchanged where edges were already correct and corrected only where an edge was inverted: no correctly-derived outcome changes.

context-validate (and context-validate-relations) passes against the active substrate after the fix and after any edge migration, with no new integrity findings; optionally a validate-time check flags any orientation-bearing edge whose stored direction violates its declared roles.

Tests assert both sides: a backwards ordering edge rejected + forwards accepted; the wildcard item_derived_from_item orientation enforced; the metadata-absent gate holds (no enforcement when orientation metadata is absent); a wrong-end read errors/signals rather than returning silent-empty; the deriver continues to treat {parent=prerequisite, child=dependent} correctly.
Build, check, and the full test suite pass at zero failures with the change in dist/.

tasks.description: Backfill decision derivation edges, then raise decision-shows-derivation to error. The decision-shows-derivation requires-edge invariant (the dedicated structural bite for the derive-decisions-from-facts convention) is live at severity WARNING: every decision must carry a decision_derived_from_item edge (the fact its resolution derives from) OR a decision_escalates_underdetermined edge (a genuinely-underdetermined escalated choice -> a framework-gap). 17 existing decisions currently warn. This task: (1) for each warning decision, read its reasoning and either edge decision_derived_from_item -> the substrate fact its resolution derived from (a research / convention / gap / prior-decision / rationale item; file a rationale item to reify a code/mandate fact that has no existing item), or decision_escalates_underdetermined -> the framework-gap capturing a genuinely-underdetermined choice; (2) once context-validate shows zero decision-shows-derivation warnings, raise the invariant severity from warning to error via amend-config. This completes the convention-articulation clean-after-backfill pattern for decision derivation, making a fork-as-decision (a decision with no shown derivation basis) UNFILEABLE.

tasks.description: Schema version-history audit (the `git log <file>` analog): a read-only command (e.g. `read-schema-history --schemaName <name>`) that prints the schema's history — which migrations ran, when, and from what base — plus the two recording fixes that make the history complete: stamp an applied-at time when a migration runs, and keep a pointer from each new baseline to the one it replaced. Without those two, the command could only show a partial story and "when" would still require digging through git commits: today the only timestamp on a migration decl is its authoring time, and each baseline advance overwrites the prior {content_hash, version} with no parent pointer (the replaced bodies sit unreferenced in the object store). The command composes what the substrate records: the registered migration decls for the schema (from→to, kind, author, authored-at, applied-at), the baseline lineage, and the installed/catalog versions.

tasks.description: Walk the migration chain incrementally (the `git bisect` analog): a read-only command (e.g. `walk-migration-chain --schemaName <name>`) that applies the schema's migration chain one step at a time in memory — apply migration 1, validate, apply migration 2, validate — to isolate which step breaks. Per step it reports the step's decl (from→to, kind) and which items' content changed; validation runs against the target schema on each cumulative prefix, attributing the first failure to the step whose application introduced it. Nothing is written — the walk is entirely in memory over the live block data and the resolved chain (the catalog chain, or the pinned pending-blocked chain when the schema is blocked). Limitation stated honestly: the catalog ships only the latest schema body per kind, so a step's output cannot be validated against an intermediate schema version (only against the final target, or an intermediate body that happens to be pinned in the object store); the op isolates the breaking transform step, which is the operator's ask.

tasks.description: Add a write-time edge kind/category validator. This hoists the existing post-hoc kind check (validateContext, context-sdk.ts:1987-2021, FGAP-086/DEC-0037) to the write surface (the raw appendRelationsForDir, context.ts:649, cannot resolve endpoint kinds under the documented layering at context.ts:617-625). Presence-gate on source_kinds/target_kinds exactly as the validate-time check (context-sdk.ts:1990) so currently-passing writes are unaffected. Resolves the write-time-enforcement half of FGAP-090. Distinct from TASK-027 (ordering-edge endpoint-role DIRECTION, ordering-only; this checks source/target KIND + category across all relation_types).
acceptance_criteria: A real append-relation CLI invocation writing an edge whose parent or child item kind is not in the relation_type's source_kinds/target_kinds is rejected at write with an error naming the offending endpoint kind + relation_type, and the edge is not persisted.

A relation_type with neither source_kinds nor target_kinds set is not rejected at write (the presence-gate at context-sdk.ts:1990 is preserved at the write surface).

Write-time and validate-time kind semantics agree: an edge the write rejects, validateContext (context-sdk.ts:1987-2021) would also flag; an edge the write accepts, validateContext does not flag for kind. The existing validateContext check is unchanged.
npm run build + check + full test pass with 0 failures; a fresh adversarial probe confirms no over-reject of presence-gated (unset-kinds) types and write/validate parity.

tasks.description: Extend `currentState` (packages/pi-context/src/context-sdk.ts:682-825) — the derivation behind context-current-state / `/context status` — so task readiness honors `task_gated_by_item` gates, not only `task_depends_on_task`. A planned task carrying a `task_gated_by_item` edge to a target NOT in the `complete` bucket must be reported blocked (target id in `blockedBy`) and excluded from `nextActions`; a gate target reaching the `complete` bucket releases the gate. Gate satisfaction reuses `bucket(target) === "complete"` via resolveStatusVocabulary (status-vocab.ts) — kind-general, the same mechanism the status-consistency invariant engine uses (context-sdk.ts:2186-2227); NOT a net-new completeness check. Both consumption sites fold gate parents: `blocked` (incompleteDeps→depParentsOf, :727-730/:742-751) and the `nextActions` topo predecessor function (:790). Scope is strictly the literal relation_type `task_gated_by_item`; `decision_gated_by_item` and non-task `*_gated_by_item` are inert (currentState buckets only tasks) and OUT of scope — the config-driven generalization is FEAT-004. NOW slice of FGAP-061.
acceptance_criteria: A planned (non-`complete`-bucket) task with a `task_gated_by_item` edge to a target NOT in the `complete` bucket is reported in `currentState().blocked` with that target id present in its `blockedBy`, and is absent from `nextActions`.

When the gate target reaches the `complete` bucket (gap→`closed`, decision→`enacted`, feature→`complete`, task→`completed`), the same task is reported ready (in `nextActions`, absent from `blocked`). Gate satisfaction is implemented as `bucket(target) === "complete"` via resolveStatusVocabulary — no kind-special-casing.

A task with BOTH `task_depends_on_task` and `task_gated_by_item`: `blockedBy` is the UNION of unsatisfied dep-parents and unsatisfied gate-targets; with no gate edges present, `currentState` output is byte-identical to current behavior (no regression to dependency derivation).
`nextActions` topo ordering excludes gate-blocked tasks — the topo predecessor function folds gate parents alongside dep parents (:790), so a gate-blocked task is never emitted as a next action.
A dangling `task_gated_by_item` target (id resolves to no item) is treated as satisfied (non-blocking), mirroring the dangling-dep guard (:732) — no throw, no block.
Scope confinement: `decision_gated_by_item` and any non-`task_gated_by_item` edge do NOT alter `currentState` output; the gate filter matches the literal `task_gated_by_item`.

A gate target in a terminal-abandoned status (`wontfix`/`superseded`/`cancelled`) keeps the gated task blocked (per criterion 2's `=== "complete"` rule); the `currentState` JSDoc (:674-680) documents this as the FEAT-004 refinement boundary.

New tests in `context-sdk.test.ts` (the `currentState` describe, :2604-2745, cloning the `task_depends_on_task` template at :2633-2678) cover: gate-blocked, gate-released-on-completion, cross-kind gate targets, dep+gate union, dangling gate target, terminal-abandoned gate target.

Gates green with full output (no pipe-to-tail): `npm run build`, `npm run check`, `npm test`. Runtime demo: real `context-current-state` (CLI) against a /tmp substrate showing a gated task as blocked, then ready after the gate target is completed — not a mocked assertion. Fresh-context adversarial probe on the gate logic (over-block, under-block, kind-leak into non-task gates, topo/`blocked` parity).

docs-surface-sync: extend the `currentState` JSDoc (:674-680) and the `context-current-state` op `description`/`promptSnippet` (ops-registry.ts:1154-1167) to state gate-aware readiness (usage-only, what it does); regen SKILL.md if the op string changed; add a CHANGELOG `[Unreleased]` entry.

`blockersOf` de-duplicates the dep+gate union, preserving discovery order (deps before gates): a target appearing in both the unsatisfied-dependency and unsatisfied-gate sets, or a redundant exact-duplicate `task_depends_on_task` edge, is listed once in `blockedBy`. Exact-duplicate edges are not producible through the write API (`appendRelations` skips an existing `(parent, child, relation_type)` triple, context.ts:649-663), so on every API-produced substrate this dedup is invisible and the single-edge no-gate path stays byte-identical to the prior derivation; on a hand-duplicated-edge substrate it collapses the redundant blocker to a single entry — a strict improvement, not a dependency-derivation regression.

tasks.description: Make the operator pi-context binary a publish-free local copy of the working tree, immune to dev builds. Today /opt/homebrew/bin/pi-context is an npm link symlink into packages/pi-context-cli/dist/, so npm run build (rm -rf dist && tsc) repoints and transiently removes the live operator binary. Retire the link; install the operator binary as a packed copy of the working-tree CLI + its @davidorex dependency set (current code, not the stale registry 0.31.0); add a promote:cli root npm-task fronting scripts/promote-cli.mjs (build, pack the set, install into the global prefix, verify a non-symlink copy), fitting the version:* / release:* composer convention. Scope: the binary coupling only — not the proving-ground harness, a CI gate, or schema-evolution work.
acceptance_criteria: realpath of /opt/homebrew/bin/pi-context resolves under /opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/@davidorex/pi-context-cli/, not under /Users/david/Projects/workflowsPiExtension.
npm ls -g --link lists no @davidorex/pi-context-cli link to the repo.
The installed operator reflects working-tree code, not the registry 0.31.0 release.
A repo npm run build leaves the realpath target of /opt/homebrew/bin/pi-context unchanged in inode and content hash.
After a repo npm run build, pi-context --version exits 0.
With packages/pi-context-cli/dist deleted, pi-context --help exits 0.
A re-promote after a repo source edit changes the content hash of the realpath target of /opt/homebrew/bin/pi-context.
No file under the repo instructs npm link for the operator binary.
packages/pi-context-cli/README.md and the root README.md document the copy install of the operator.

The real-global prefix is resolved non-poisonably: an inherited npm_config_prefix (which `npm run promote:cli`, the documented invocation, always injects as the true global) does NOT divert the resolved or installed target — the `npm prefix -g` probe and the `npm i -g` install both run under an env with every npm_config_* scrubbed, so the resolved prefix reflects the true global regardless of any inherited value. The inherited prefix is NOT refused: refusing it would break the tool's own documented path and provide no protection the scrub does not, since npm's legitimate injection and a hostile override are indistinguishable at the env-var level.
Every global install or removal the promote performs acts only on the validated target prefix.
A target prefix whose realpath resolves to or under the repo root is refused — no global install or removal occurs.
A failed global install leaves the prior operator runnable and its installed payload unchanged.
Invoking the promote with the argument token --prefix=<dir> installs the operator at <dir>/bin/pi-context.
tasks.notes: Addresses issue-004. Scope: the binary coupling only.

tasks.description: Add a load-time config-migration path to loadConfigForDir (context.ts:565-582): read the config's schema_version and, on mismatch with the bundled config schema version, walk the schema-name-keyed migration chain for config before validating — mirroring validateBlockWithMigrationForDir (schema-validator.ts:205-242), including its fail-fast throw when no chain resolves. Add a config migration registry entry and the declarative-transform primitive (map_each) that maps array elements within a migration step.
acceptance_criteria: loadConfigForDir reads the config's schema_version, compares it to the bundled config schema version, and on mismatch consults the schema-name-keyed migration chain for `config` (via the project MigrationRegistry built from migrations.json) and runs it forward before validating; on mismatch with no resolvable chain it fails fast (throws, mirroring validateBlockWithMigrationForDir's version-mismatch throw), never falling back to raw validation; on version match it does not consult the registry.

A `config` migration declaration covering the on-disk config's schema_version up to the bundled schema version is filed in the substrate's migrations.json via write-schema-migration, such that loadConfigForDir resolves a NON-EMPTY chain for it; the registry is not empty for `config` at the demonstrated version gap.

A config whose schema_version lags the bundled config schema by the real on-disk gap (1.0.0 → current) loads valid through the migrated path, carried forward in memory, with zero config-load failure of any exit class — demonstrated by runtime reproduction on a scratch substrate, not by a version-matched no-op.

The map_each declarative-transform primitive is added to the TransformOp union (migrations-store.ts:64-68), the migrations.schema.json TransformOp oneOf, and applyOp (migration-registry-loader.ts), with two declared modes: table mode (`path` + `table` [+ `fallback`, enum parent|child, defaulting to "parent" when absent]) replaces each string element of the array at `path` by table[element], or by {relation_type: element, item_endpoint: fallback} when unmatched, with non-string elements passing through unchanged; and set-on-each mode (`path` + `field` + `value`) sets element[field]=value on each object element of the array at `path`. A migration step exercising each mode transforms the array elements at `path` accordingly, verified against a fixture.
A current-version config (schema_version equal to the bundled schema version) loads byte-unchanged: no migration applied, registry not consulted.
TASK-070 is recorded as the config instance of FEAT-010; its scope is the config instance, not the block-data class.

samples/migrations.json ships an identity `config` migration declaration covering 1.0.0 → the bundled config schema version; a fresh init → accept-all → install substrate carries that declaration in its migrations.json, so loadConfigForDir resolves a NON-EMPTY chain for `config` and a catalog-born substrate loads through the migration path, never reaching the no-chain throw.

tasks.description: Add a sanctioned config-repair path that applies the corrective mutation and validates the result, rather than loading-and-validating the stored config before the repair, so an already-invalid config is recoverable. Scope: any invalid config regardless of version, distinct from the version-gap migration. The repair mechanism is a `force`/`repair` opt on amend-config that bypasses the pre-load validation and validates the result via the existing write path (block-api.ts:892); derived from the existing op surface, no DEC.
acceptance_criteria: An invalid config, including schema_version equal to current with an invalid body, is restored to validity through a sanctioned CLI op without a forbidden direct edit of config.json.
The repair validates the corrected result, not the pre-existing stored config; it does not throw on load before the mutation applies.
Both the version-gap subset and the no-version-delta invalidity are recoverable.

The repair is a `force`/`repair` mode of the existing amend-config op — the same config write, mirroring its existing dryRun opt (context.ts:1229): it skips the pre-load validation (context.ts:1251) and relies on amend-config's existing pre-write result-validation (block-api.ts:892-895). It is an opt on amend-config, not a separate recover op: config-repair is a validation-skip mode of an existing write, not a missing library capability, so the opt is derived from the existing amend-config surface.
tasks.notes: Repair mechanism is DERIVED from the existing op surface, not deferred to a DECISION. tasks.title: Birth-relations entries afford the role-typed orientation form — filing atoms usable for all 17 role-bearing relation_types, with preview parity
tasks.description: Give the birth-relations entry both orientation forms, single-sourcing guard + mapping in orientAppendInput (per FGAP-121 proposed_resolution): each entry carries {relation_type, other, ordinal?} plus EITHER direction: as_parent|as_child (raw form, unchanged semantics — the NEW item occupies that endpoint) OR role: primary|counter (role form — the NEW item holds that role; other holds the complement), mutually exclusive per entry, enforced at coerceBirthRelations before any write. appendBirthRelations maps role entries to the role-typed RelationAppendInput (primary → {primary: itemId, counter: other}; counter → {primary: other, counter: itemId}) and delegates to appendRelationByRef unchanged, so the existing orientAppendInput errors apply verbatim: role on a role-less relation → the existing no-role_direction throw; direction on an ambiguous role-bearing relation → the existing re-issue throw, now followable in-shape. Preview parity: upsert-block-item --dryRun runs the orientation mapping/guard over coerced entries (orientation needs only the registry; endpoint resolution stays out — the item is unwritten), so a preview refuses what the live run refuses. Docs-surface-sync: both op strings in ops-registry.ts (descriptions + relations param/entry descriptions), packages/pi-context/README.md write-pipeline + Block CRUD prose, skill-narrative.md atomic-filing paragraph (both entry forms stated), SKILL.md regen, CHANGELOG [Unreleased], CLAUDE.md canonical filing pattern in lockstep.
acceptance_criteria: A birth entry with role: primary|counter files an orientation-ambiguous role-typed relation (e.g. decision_derived_from_item) atomically with the item, and the stored edge orientation matches the relation's declared role_direction (read back and asserted).

Guard-path regression locks: ambiguous relation + direction form → refused with the existing re-issue error; role form on a role-less relation → refused with the existing no-role_direction error; an entry carrying both or neither of direction/role → refused at coercion before any write; all refusals byte-restored (mid-composite all-or-nothing).
